concerns.text: The core validation metric 'discrepancy' (Planned Power - Achieved Power) is constructed using the 'observed effect size' as a direct input for the 'Achieved Power' calculation. Validating whether 'Planned Power' is accurate by comparing it to a value derived from the *observed* effect size is circular and statistically invalid. Observed effect sizes in underpowered studies are subject to the 'Winner's Curse' (systematic overestimation). Using an overestimated observed effect to calculate 'Achieved Power' will artificially inflate the achieved power, thereby shrinking the discrepancy metric and masking the true overestimation in the planned power. The validation target is not independent of the noise in the predictor (observed effect).

The methodology for calculating 'achieved_power' using the 'observed_effect_size' is methodologically unsound for the stated research question. In statistical power analysis, 'achieved power' (post-hoc power) calculated from the observed effect size is a deterministic function of the p-value and provides no new information about the study's sensitivity. It is a known statistical identity that post-hoc power is perfectly correlated with the p-value. Using this as a dependent variable to test for 'systematic overestimation' of *planned* power is a category error; it tests whether the observed p-value was significant, not whether the initial power analysis was accurate. The spec fails to distinguish between 'post-hoc power' (invalid for this purpose) and 'sensitivity analysis' (power to detect the *assumed* effect size with the *actual* N).

The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test is proposed to test the discrepancy distribution against a null hypothesis of zero mean. The KS test is designed to compare two distributions (or a distribution against a reference distribution), not to test a mean against a specific value. A one-sample t-test or a Wilcoxon signed-rank test would be the appropriate statistical method for testing if the mean discrepancy differs from zero. Using KS for this purpose is a methodological mismatch.
